answers.field_label: Present Land Use Zoning General Plan Designation
answers.value:
Existing Land Use: Predominantly irrigated row crops (alfalfa and tomatoes) with a single rural residence and accessory agricultural outbuildings; portions of the site are fallow and used for equipment storage.

Existing Zoning: AG-20 (Agriculture, 20-acre minimum parcel size) and UR (Urban Reserve) under the City of Riverbend Zoning Ordinance.

Existing General Plan Land Use Designation: Primarily Urban Reserve and Agriculture with a small portion designated Low Density Residential (2–4 du/ac) near the eastern boundary and a designated future collector roadway corridor along the southern project edge.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Specific Plan ("Project") is a comprehensive, master-planned development on approximately 185.4 acres located on the western urban edge of the City of Riverbend in Sacramento County. The Project is intended to implement the City’s long-range growth strategy by creating a walkable, transit-supportive neighborhood that blends a variety of housing types with neighborhood-serving commercial, office, civic, and recreational uses.

Residential Development (approx. 92.3 acres, up to 725 units):
The Project proposes a mix of single-family detached, small-lot, townhome, and multifamily units organized into distinct neighborhoods around a central village core. Approximately 325 units would be traditional single-family detached homes on lots generally ranging from 4,000 to 6,000 square feet. Another approximately 250 units would be small-lot and attached townhomes arranged in clusters with shared motor courts and common open space areas. Up to 150 multifamily units would be provided in two- to three-story garden-style buildings located near transit stops and the village commercial center.

The residential neighborhoods are designed with a connected street grid, short blocks, and an extensive network of sidewalks and Class I multi-use paths. Each neighborhood would include small pocket parks or greens within a 5-minute walk of most homes. The Project anticipates a buildout population of approximately 2,000 residents, with a targeted mix of incomes and household types, including young families, downsizing seniors, and workforce households employed in the Riverbend area.

Commercial and Office Uses (approx. 25.7 acres):
The village core, located near the intersection of West Harper Road and the planned north-south collector, would include approximately 145,000 square feet of commercial space and 85,000 square feet of office space. Commercial uses are envisioned to include a neighborhood grocery store (approximately 40,000 square feet), small-format retail shops, restaurants, personal services (e.g., dry cleaner, salon, fitness studio), and a small medical clinic. Office space would be provided in two- to three-story multi-tenant buildings intended to accommodate professional services firms, medical offices, and small technology or creative businesses.

The commercial and office areas are designed with pedestrian-oriented frontages, outdoor seating areas, and landscaped plazas. On-street parking and shared parking lots behind buildings would serve local patrons and employees. The commercial/office component is projected to support approximately 580 permanent jobs at full buildout.

Civic, Educational, and Recreational Facilities:
The Project includes a 3.5-acre site reserved for a future public elementary school or joint-use educational facility, anticipated to be developed by the local school district as enrollment demand warrants. A separate 2.8-acre site is designated for a multi-purpose civic building that could accommodate a community center, branch library, and flexible meeting spaces for neighborhood associations, youth programs, and senior activities.

Recreational amenities include a 12-acre community park with multi-purpose sports fields, children’s play areas, picnic shelters, and a small amphitheater for outdoor events. In addition, the Project provides approximately 10 acres of greenways and linear parks along the internal trail system and preserved drainage corridors. These areas would include interpretive signage about local habitat, native plantings, and informal seating areas.

Transportation and Circulation Improvements:
The Project would construct an internal hierarchy of complete streets, including a primary north-south collector road with landscaped medians, traffic-calming roundabouts, and Class II on-street bicycle lanes. Local residential streets would be designed with traffic-calming features such as narrow travel lanes, street trees, bulb-outs, and raised crosswalks at key pedestrian crossings.

A shared-use Class I multi-use path would run generally east-west through the site, ultimately connecting to the City’s broader trail network and the Feather River regional trail. The Project also proposes enhanced transit stops along West Harper Road and within the village core, with shelters, seating, and real-time transit information to support future bus service expansion.

Infrastructure, Utilities, and Water Facilities:
Potable water service would be provided by the City of Riverbend via a new 12-inch water main looped through the Project and connecting to an existing transmission main located in West Harper Road. The Project’s estimated maximum day demand is approximately 0.45 million gallons per day (MGD), which would be met through existing City surface water supplies and treatment capacity.

Sanitary sewer flows would be conveyed via a gravity collection system to an existing trunk sewer located east of the site, with minor off-site improvements to increase capacity at key segments. Stormwater management would employ a combination of low-impact development (LID) features—such as bioretention planters, permeable paving in selected parking areas, and vegetated swales—along with a system of detention basins and water quality treatment basins located in the southern and western portions of the site. These facilities are designed to meet the City’s stormwater quality requirements and maintain post-project runoff rates at or below existing conditions for the 2-, 10-, and 100-year storm events.

Energy, Sustainability, and Community Solar:
The Project incorporates a strong emphasis on energy efficiency and sustainability. All new buildings are expected to meet or exceed current Title 24 energy standards, and the Specific Plan encourages the use of all-electric building design for multifamily and office structures where feasible. Rooftop solar photovoltaic (PV) systems would be offered as a standard or optional feature on residential units and would be strongly encouraged for commercial and office buildings.

In addition, a 1.5-acre community solar garden is proposed near the southern edge of the site, with a planned capacity of approximately 3.2 megawatts (MW). This facility would be developed in partnership with a third-party solar provider and could offer subscription-based renewable energy to project residents and businesses, reducing greenhouse gas emissions and supporting local climate action goals.

Phasing and Construction:
Buildout of the Project is anticipated over a 10- to 15-year period, with initial phases focusing on backbone infrastructure, the first residential neighborhoods, and a portion of the village commercial area. Subsequent phases would complete the remaining residential areas, community park, trail network, and civic/educational sites. Construction activities would typically occur during standard daytime hours, with best management practices implemented to minimize dust, noise, and traffic disruptions.

Overall Objectives:
Key objectives of the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Specific Plan include: (1) providing much-needed housing in a range of types and price points to help address regional housing shortages; (2) creating a walkable, bike-friendly neighborhood that reduces reliance on single-occupant vehicle trips; (3) supporting local economic development by introducing new commercial and office opportunities; (4) integrating parks, open space, and habitat features into the community fabric; and (5) implementing the City’s General Plan vision for a compact, sustainable growth pattern on the western urban edge while preserving high-value agricultural and habitat lands elsewhere in the Planning Area.
